text: The patient is a fifty-seven year old male. Seven months ago, he underwent Hartmann's operation due to a transanal low rectal penetrating injury caused by a steel beam that went through his anus and into the pelvic cavity. Operation findings showed rectal perforation along with mesenteric tearing of the sigmoid colon mesentery.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy. After skin preparation and draping, the surgeon deepens the colostomy into the abdominal cavity, creating an access port wide enough for a glove port insertion. The glove port is inserted, and the colonic limb is mobilized.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to lyse extensive adhesions between the abdominal wall and the small bowel and colonic mesentery. After exploration, the team assesses the risk and decides against proceeding with the operation using only a single port.

The surgeon performs laparoscopic dissection of small bowel adhesions, a challenging step in Hartmann's reversal. An additional 5mm trocar is inserted into the right lower abdomen, later serving as a drain port.

The surgeon uses endoshears for meticulous dissection of a segment of the small bowel extensively adhered to the colonic mesentery. After mobilizing the colon, the surgical team proceeds to the pelvic floor, where they identify the rectal stump and clear nearby adhesions using sharp dissection.

After completing sufficient dissection, the surgeon inserts an anvil through the glove port and a circular stapler through the anus. The stapler penetrates the rectal mucosa, docks with the anvil, and is fired to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon conducts an air leakage test and intraoperative endoscopic evaluation to assess the integrity of the anastomosis, confirming no signs of leakage, indicating the completion of the operation.

In summary, the operation lasted one hundred and eighty minutes. Expected blood loss was about one hundred cc's. The patient was given an oral diet on post-operative day six and was discharged on day nine without any complications.
